title: Linguistics
intro: Linguistics questions in this unit cluster heavily around a small set of foundational scholars and the theories named for them. Noam Chomsky is the single most-asked answerline, reflecting his centrality to generative grammar, Universal Grammar, and the innateness debate; he appears both under his own name and as "Avram Noam Chomsky." William Labov follows closely, anchoring the unit's sociolinguistics content through his variationist studies of Martha's Vineyard and New York City department stores. Ferdinand de Saussure, Edward Sapir, Benjamin Lee Whorf, George Lakoff, and Steven Pinker round out the roster of frequently cited theorists, with the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis of linguistic relativity appearing as a distinct high-frequency answerline in its own right.

Below the level of individual scholars, the canon distributes fairly evenly across the traditional core subfields. Phonetics and phonology contribute answerlines such as vowels, phonemes, tones, aspiration, vowel harmony, and click consonants, alongside transcription and theoretical apparatus like the International Phonetic Alphabet and optimality theory. Morphology supplies word classes (nouns, verbs, adjectives, pronouns) and grammatical categories (number, gender, case, evidentiality), while syntax contributes structural concepts including binding, X-bar theory, recursion, ellipsis, and word order. Semantics and pragmatics are represented by deictics, metaphor, politeness theory, and cross-linguistic color terminology.

A substantial share of questions test specific languages and language families rather than abstract theory. Japanese, Arabic, English, Sanskrit, French, Korean, and Spanish are the most frequently asked individual languages, while Celtic, Germanic, Romance, and Bantu languages recur as family-level answerlines alongside language isolates like Basque and Navajo. Historical and comparative linguistics is represented by Proto-Indo-European, the comparative method, and named sound laws such as Grimm's Law and Grassmann's Law, reflecting the canon's continued emphasis on Indo-European reconstruction.

A smaller but consistent cluster covers language contact and variation: code switching, African American Vernacular English, diglossia, and heritage language on the sociolinguistic side, and pidgins, creoles, Tok Pisin, Esperanto, and sign languages on the contact and constructed-language side. First and second language acquisition, Universal Grammar, and poverty-of-the-stimulus arguments round out a recurring psycholinguistic thread tied to the unit's emphasis on Chomskyan theory.

sections.name: Key Linguists and Theories
sections.blurb: The scholars whose names anchor major linguistic hypotheses, from generative grammar to linguistic relativity.

entries.note: American linguist who founded generative grammar and Universal Grammar; wrote Syntactic Structures and critiqued behaviorist theories of language.
Sociolinguist who pioneered quantitative variationist studies, including the Martha's Vineyard and New York City department store research.
Swiss linguist and founder of structuralism who distinguished langue from parole and signifier from signified.
Idea that language shapes or constrains thought and perception, associated with Edward Sapir and Benjamin Lee Whorf.
Full name of the linguist behind generative grammar, Universal Grammar, and the minimalist program in syntax.
American linguist who studied Hopi and argued language influences thought, co-namesake of the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis.
American linguist and anthropologist who studied Native American languages and co-developed linguistic relativity with Whorf.
Cognitive linguist known for conceptual metaphor theory, co-author of Metaphors We Live By with Mark Johnson.
Short-form reference to George Lakoff, cognitive linguist known for conceptual metaphor theory and framing research.
Full name of the cognitive psychologist and linguist known for The Language Instinct and nativist views on acquisition.
Cognitive scientist who popularized Chomskyan linguistics in The Language Instinct and argued for an innate language faculty.
Sociolinguist famous for the Martha's Vineyard and New York City department store studies on sound change and social stratification.

sections.name: Phonetics, Phonology, and Writing Systems
sections.blurb: The sounds of speech, how they pattern into systems, and the scripts and frameworks used to describe them.

entries.note: Speech sounds produced without obstruction of airflow, classified by tongue height, backness, and lip rounding.
Smallest contrastive sound units in a language whose substitution changes meaning, identified via minimal pairs.
Subfield studying how speech sounds pattern within a language, including processes like assimilation and epenthesis.
Phonological framework in which surface forms emerge from ranked, violable constraints rather than ordered rules.
Pitch patterns that distinguish word meaning in tonal languages such as Mandarin and many African languages.
Puff of air accompanying certain consonants, phonemically contrastive in Hindi and Sanskrit but allophonic in English.
Phonological process requiring vowels within a word to share features like backness, found in Turkish and Finnish.
Consonants produced with a suction airstream mechanism, found in Khoisan languages and in Zulu.
Common-link answerline on rhotic consonants, whose realization as trill, tap, or approximant varies across languages.
Presence or absence of pronounced r sounds, a key variable distinguishing dialects like American from British English.
Units of speech organization built around a nucleus, typically a vowel, flanked by onset and coda consonants.
Use of pitch to distinguish lexical or grammatical meaning, as in Mandarin, Vietnamese, and many African languages.
Writing system in which consonant-vowel sequences are written as units with vowels marked by diacritics, as in Devanagari.
Consonants produced by forcing air through a narrow channel, creating turbulence, such as f, s, and sh.
International Phonetic Alphabet, a standardized system of symbols for transcribing the sounds of any spoken language.
Articulators used to produce labial and bilabial sounds; common-link answerline tying together lip-articulated sounds.
Phonological process altering sounds at word or morpheme boundaries, as in Sanskrit's extensive sandhi rules.
The mid-central unstressed vowel sound, the most common vowel in English, often written as an inverted e.

sections.name: Morphology and Grammatical Categories
sections.blurb: How words are built from smaller units and how languages grammatically mark categories like number, gender, and case.

entries.note: Word class denoting actions, states, or occurrences, central to predicate structure across languages.
Smallest meaningful units of language, combining as roots and affixes to build words.
Word class substituting for nouns or noun phrases, including personal, demonstrative, and relative types.
Grammatical category marking quantity distinctions such as singular, plural, and dual across nouns and verbs.
Morphological process modifying a word to express categories like tense, case, number, or gender.
Grammatical case marking the direct object of a transitive verb in many inflected languages.
Word class modifying nouns by describing their qualities or properties.
Grammatical category marking the source of information for a statement, obligatory in Quechua and Turkish.
Noun classification system, often masculine, feminine, or neuter, that triggers agreement on adjectives and verbs.
Word class denoting people, places, things, or concepts, typically inflecting for number and case.
Morphological process repeating all or part of a word to mark categories like plurality or intensity.
Morphological type where words form by stringing together clearly segmentable affixes, typical of Turkish and Korean.
Grammatical number category marking exactly two entities, distinct from singular and plural, found in Sanskrit and Arabic.
Grammatical noun-class system, often masculine, feminine, or neuter, requiring agreement with adjectives and verbs.
System of noun and pronoun inflection marking grammatical role, as in Latin's nominative, genitive, dative, and accusative.
Theoretical concept describing how one member of a linguistic opposition is more basic or unmarked than the other.
Subfield studying the internal structure of words, including roots, stems, and affixes.
Common-link answerline on numeral systems and how languages express counting and grammatical number.
Verbs that take a direct object, contrasted with intransitive verbs.
Word class denoting actions or states, central to predicate and tense/aspect marking.

sections.name: Syntax
sections.blurb: The rules governing how words combine into phrases and sentences.

entries.note: Common-link answerline on interrogative sentence formation, including wh-movement and yes/no question structures across languages.
Subfield studying how words combine into phrases and sentences according to structural rules.
Syntactic theory governing the distribution of pronouns, reflexives, and anaphora within a sentence.
Chomskyan framework positing a uniform phrase structure template with head, complement, and specifier positions.
Syntactic phenomenon in which understood material is omitted from a sentence, as in "I can, and you can too."
Property allowing structures to embed within structures of the same type, cited by Chomsky as a hallmark of language.
Common-link answerline on the system of rules governing a language's structure, spanning syntax and morphology.
The word that determines the grammatical category and properties of a phrase, central to X-bar theory.
Syntactic and semantic process of denying or reversing the truth of a proposition, marked variously across languages.
Syntactic construction in which the subject of an embedded clause appears as the subject of a higher verb.
Typological classification of languages by the relative order of subject, verb, and object.

sections.name: Semantics and Pragmatics
sections.blurb: How meaning is encoded in language and shaped by context, inference, and figurative thought.

entries.note: Subfield studying how context, speaker intention, and inference shape meaning beyond literal sentence content.
Subfield studying the literal meaning of words, phrases, and sentences.
Words like "here," "now," and "I" whose reference depends entirely on the context of utterance.
Figurative mappings between conceptual domains, studied in George Lakoff's conceptual metaphor theory.
Common-link answerline on cross-linguistic color terminology, studied since Berlin and Kay's universal color-term hierarchy.
Nonverbal communicative movements studied alongside speech in multimodal and pragmatic research on meaning-making.
Pragmatic theory, notably Brown and Levinson's face-saving framework, describing strategies for softening face-threatening speech.
Common-link answerline on how languages encode temporal reference through tense, aspect, and time adverbials.

sections.name: Historical and Comparative Linguistics
sections.blurb: Sound change, reconstruction, and the family trees connecting related languages over time.

entries.note: Reconstructed ancestor of the Indo-European family, recovered via the comparative method from Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin.
Sound-shift pattern describing systematic consonant changes from Proto-Indo-European to Proto-Germanic, formulated by Jacob Grimm.
Technique reconstructing proto-languages by systematically comparing sound correspondences among related languages.
Sound law describing dissimilation of aspirated consonants in Sanskrit and Ancient Greek, formulated by Hermann Grassmann.

sections.name: Sociolinguistics
sections.blurb: Variation in language tied to community, identity, social class, and context of use.

entries.note: Alternation between two or more languages or varieties within a single conversation by bilingual or multilingual speakers.
Systematic English variety with distinctive phonological, grammatical, and lexical features, studied extensively by William Labov.
Common-link answerline on sociolinguistic findings, notably Labov's, that women often lead change toward prestige forms.
Common-link answerline evoking Basil Bernstein's theory of restricted and elaborated speech codes tied to social class.
Situation in which two language varieties, a "high" and a "low" form, are used in complementary social functions.
A language learned at home from family that is not the dominant language of the surrounding society.
Site of William Labov's landmark study linking vowel centralization to local identity and resistance to outside influence.
Social value attached to a language variety that influences which forms speakers adopt or avoid.

sections.name: Language Acquisition and Psycholinguistics
sections.blurb: How children and adults acquire language and the mental processes and debates behind it.

entries.note: Process by which children naturally acquire their native language, central to debates over innateness.
General term for how humans acquire language, whether as a first or subsequent language.
Study of how people learn a language after their first, examining input, age effects, and interference.
Argument that children acquire grammar despite limited and imperfect input, used to support innate Universal Grammar.
Chomsky's proposed innate linguistic structure shared by all humans that constrains the grammars children can acquire.

sections.name: Language Contact, Creoles, and Constructed Languages
sections.blurb: Languages that arise from contact between speech communities, plus languages deliberately invented rather than naturally evolved.

entries.note: Loan translations in which a foreign expression's parts are translated literally into the borrowing language.
Stable natural languages that develop from the nativization of a pidgin, acquiring full grammatical complexity.
Constructed international auxiliary language created by L. L. Zamenhof in 1887 to facilitate global communication.
Manual-visual languages with full grammatical structure, such as American Sign Language, distinct from spoken modality.
English-based creole and official language of Papua New Guinea, developed from a Pacific trade pidgin.
Natural languages that emerge when a pidgin becomes the native language of a speech community.
Words borrowed from one language into another, often adapted to the borrowing language's phonology and morphology.
Simplified contact languages that develop for limited communication between groups without a shared language, lacking native speakers.

sections.name: Language Families and Individual Languages
sections.blurb: Specific languages and language families that recur as answerlines, spanning every inhabited continent.

entries.note: East Asian language with subject-object-verb order, agglutinative morphology, and multiple honorific speech levels.
Semitic language with templatic root-and-pattern morphology, official across the Middle East and North Africa.
West Germanic language with limited inflection, relatively fixed word order, and heavy French and Latin borrowing.
Classical Indo-Aryan language of Hindu scripture, with an elaborate case system and grammar codified by Panini.
Romance language descended from Latin, official in France and much of Francophone Africa and Canada.
Continent home to hundreds of indigenous Aboriginal languages alongside Australian English.
East Asian language with agglutinative morphology and the Hangul writing system, often classified as a language isolate.
Romance language descended from Latin, the most widely spoken Romance language, official across Spain and Latin America.
Indo-European language of Homer and classical Greece, with rich case, aspect, and dialectal variation.
Indo-European branch including Irish, Welsh, and Scottish Gaelic, known for initial consonant mutations.
Languages descended from Vulgar Latin, including Spanish, French, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian.
Large Niger-Congo language family of sub-Saharan Africa featuring extensive noun-class systems.
Language isolate spoken in northern Spain and southern France, unrelated to any other known language family.
Official name of Australia, whose linguistic landscape includes Australian English and numerous endangered Aboriginal languages.
Indo-European branch including English, German, and the Scandinavian languages, shaped historically by Grimm's Law.
Classical Italic language of ancient Rome, ancestor of the Romance languages, with an elaborate case and conjugation system.
Athabaskan language of the American Southwest, known for complex verb morphology and use as a WWII code.
East Slavic language with a six-case system and Cyrillic script, widely spoken across Eastern Europe and Central Asia.
North Germanic language with pitch accent, spoken primarily in Sweden and parts of Finland.
Historically Germanic language written in Hebrew script, traditionally spoken by Ashkenazi Jews across Central and Eastern Europe.
